steps.title: Identify the sampling design and its origin
steps.content:
The experiment began with a key design choice: 90 students were selected at random from a certain high school.

In statistical inference, random sampling is the method that permits generalization.

The critical question is: from what population were these 90 students drawn?

The answer is stated in the problem: from that particular high school.

Random sampling allows results to be generalized only to the population from which the sample was taken.

steps.title: Evaluate what each choice represents
steps.content:
• Choice A — All students from high schools in Michigan: This is too broad. The sample came from only one school, not randomly from all Michigan schools.
  • Choice B — The 90 students who participated in the experiment: This is the sample, not a generalization target.
  • Choice C — All students from the high school: This is the source population from which the 90 were randomly selected.
  • Choice D — The 36 students who were able to correctly identify the pureed food sample while wearing nose plugs: This is a subset of the sample, not a population for generalization.

steps.title: Determine the maximum scope of valid generalization
steps.content:
The fundamental principle: a random sample generalizes to the population from which it was randomly drawn, but no further.

The 90 students were randomly selected from one high school.

Therefore, the results of the experiment can be generalized to all students in that high school.

We cannot claim the findings hold for other Michigan high schools (different populations), and we must not mistake the sample for a population or a sample subset for the target of generalization.

That confirms C is correct.
